title: DAVID — The Vowels Egyptian Never Wrote

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Coptic — the last stage of the Egyptian language, the form that finally wrote the vowels hieroglyphics never recorded, and the key that helped Champollion crack the Rosetta Stone.

For three and a half millennia, Egyptian hieroglyphics wrote consonants and left vowels to the reader. When the script died, so did the sound — until Coptic, which wrote Egyptian with the Greek alphabet and preserved what the ancient scribes omitted.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?

The proof is in the Nag Hammadi Library — fifty-two Gnostic texts found in a sealed jar in Upper Egypt in 1945, written in Coptic — and in the living liturgy of the Coptic Orthodox Church, where this language has been sung without interruption for seventeen centuries.

We read Coptic corpus-first: the Greek-based alphabet directly records vowels, making Coptic the only form of Egyptian we can fully vocalise; dialectal comparison between Sahidic, Bohairic, Fayyumic, and Akhmimic reconstructs the vowel history; and surviving Coptic liturgy provides living pronunciation continuity.

Coptic is the best-attested stage of Egyptian precisely because it wrote vowels. The Bohairic dialect survives in active liturgical use; Sahidic — the literary standard — is attested in manuscripts but no longer spoken as a vernacular. I label each claim accordingly.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED vowels · LITURGICAL survival (Bohairic) · EXTINCT vernacular

beats.speech_text: Listen — Ⲫⲓⲱⲧ ⲉⲧϩⲛ ⲙ̀ⲫⲏⲟⲩⲓ — Our Father who art in heaven — the Lord's Prayer in Bohairic Coptic, still sung in Egyptian churches today, the oldest surviving voiced form of the Egyptian language.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED TEXT · Bohairic Coptic · Lord's Prayer · living liturgy

beats.speech_text: Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
